data.title: Live updates: Houthis hit Red Sea tankers; Congress votes on separate war powers resolutions
data.description:
President Trump is threatening to intensify the war with Iran as some Republican lawmakers urge him to “finish the job,” but there is also widespread skepticism on Capitol Hill on both sides of the aisle about whether he can deliver a victory.

The House convenes Thursday morning to vote on a Democrat-sponsored war powers resolution. The Senate will vote on a separate but similar resolution. They come as Trump’s support for the Iran conflict is reaching a new low among his base.

The Iran-backed Houthi rebels in Yemen on Thursday attacked two oil tankers in the Red Sea, a ramping up of the conflict since the group announced a blockade of Saudi ships attempting to go through the Bab el-Mandeb Strait earlier this week. 

The Senate Judiciary Committee is scheduled to discuss the nomination of acting Attorney General Todd Blanche to take the role permanently. Republican Sens. John Cornyn (Texas) and Thom Tillis (N.C.) have not committed to backing him, and their opposition could sink the nomination. A vote is not expected at this point.

And Dr. Erica Schwartz faces a vote in the Senate Health, Education, Labor and Pensions (HELP) Committee for her nomination to be director of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.

The Energy Department announced Wednesday a nuclear cooperation deal with Saudi Arabia, and it drew swift backlash. Insiders can read more here.

White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt will hold a press briefing today at 11 a.m. EDT. Following, Trump is set to visit the Environmental Protection Agency and will make a statement about the Ratepayer Protection Pledge, a pledge he issued in March in which U.S. artificial intelligence (AI) companies cover the electricity costs for their data centers, not consumers.

Follow live below.

data.title: Watch live: White House holds briefing as Iran tensions fuel fears of wider conflict
data.description:
White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt will brief reporters on Thursday morning as the administration faces scrutiny over its strategy in Iran, the broader conflict in the Middle East and as the death toll rises for American troops.

President Trump has issued a slew of warnings to the Iranian regime and its allies, including Houthi rebels in Yemen, this week as tit-for-tat strikes intensify between the two sides of the nearly five-month-long war. Questions are also swirling about the recent nuclear deal framework reached by the U.S. and Saudi Arabia.

The House and Senate are also voting Thursday on separate war powers resolutions seeking to bring an end to the fighting.

The briefing is scheduled to begin at 11 a.m. EDT.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: House approves Department of War renaming in annual defense bill
data.description:
House lawmakers approved the renaming of the Defense Department to the “Department of War” in the body’s majority vote for the annual defense policy bill on Wednesday.  

The fiscal 2027 National Defense Authorization Act (NDAA) includes a $1.15 trillion spending request for national security and pay raises for service members. The legislation, which typically enjoys bipartisan support, passed by a slim margin of 216-212. 

Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth has led the call to rename the department to its previous moniker, saying at a press conference last fall that the Trump administration wanted to “raise up warriors, not just defenders.”

The department was initially called the “War Department” before former President Harry Truman signed the National Security Act of 1947, which authorized a major restructuring to the U.S. military. 

President Trump signed an executive order in September supporting the department’s return to its original name, and Hegseth directed the change on its official website, social media accounts and physical signage. However, only Congress holds the power to make the change official. 

The legislation is now headed to the upper chamber for final approval after it overcame concerns from a majority of House Democrats and some Republicans related to the Iran war and a provision to expand collaboration between the U.S. and Israeli militaries. 

GOP Rep. Thomas Massie (Ky.) and Democratic Rep. Ro Khanna (Calif.) introduced an amendment to strip Section 219 from the bill, which directs Hegseth to appoint an “executive agent” at the Pentagon to supervise this joint initiative between the U.S. and Israel. This amendment was excluded from the House’s final bill. 

Massie, who voted against the bill on Wednesday, called this year’s NDAA “a betrayal of American sovereignty” in a statement shared on social media. 

Republican Rep. Keith Self (Texas), a supporter of this legislation, called Massie’s reading of Section 219 a “tortured reading of the verbiage.”

Six Democrats crossed party lines to pass the NDAA, while seven GOP lawmakers voted against the annual defense policy on Wednesday. It faces an uphill climb in the upper chamber after Democrats blocked a similar bill just last week.

data.title: Stirewalt: Both parties should be 'ashamed' of 'incoherent immigration system'
data.description:
NewsNation’s Chris Stirewalt said Wednesday both parties “should be ashamed” of the state of the country’s immigration system. 

“Both of these parties should be ashamed of the fact that we have an incoherent, ridiculous immigration system in the United States, which, whether you think it should be more permissive or less permissive, whether you think it should be tougher or more lax, it’s been broken for a long time, and everybody knows it,” Stirewalt told host Katie Pavlich on NewsNation’s “Elizabeth Vargas Reports.”

Stirewalt pointed to Senate Republicans blocking a bipartisan immigration bill in 2024. The legislation would have accelerated the screening time for migrants arriving at the border, boosted funding for immigration enforcement agencies and expanded lawful pathways to status.

The majority of Senate Republicans voted against advancing the bill after President Trump, in the midst of his third presidential campaign, urged lawmakers to oppose it.

Stirewalt later noted that once Trump returned to office, Republican majorities in both chambers “didn’t do anything except give the president money and power for enforcement.”

The One Big Beautiful Bill Act, which received support from zero congressional Democrats, provided $170.7 billion for immigration and border enforcement to the Department of Homeland Security, Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), Customs and Border Protection, along with the Pentagon’s presence along the southern border. 

The agencies must spend the funds by Sept. 30, 2029. ICE and U.S. Border Patrol also received $64 billion combined via reconciliation legislation GOP lawmakers passed last month — which fully funded the agencies through the end of Trump’s second term.

But Congress has not passed comprehensive immigration reform in decades, despite the number of undocumented migrants reaching an all-time high of 14 million in 2023, according to the Pew Research Center.

As of April, more than 3.5 million migrants had pending removal, deportation, exclusion, asylum and withholding of removal cases, according to the Justice Department’s Executive Office for Immigration Review. 

Stirewalt, though, also blamed congressional Democrats for the immigration system, referencing their deference to former President Obama while he was in office.

“Democrats did the same thing with Barack Obama,” he added. “They didn’t fix the immigration system when they had control of both houses of Congress and the presidency. They just looked to him to do it by executive order.”

data.title: Don't wire foreign enemies into America's power grid
data.description:
The U.S. is racing to build infrastructure that will power artificial intelligence, advanced manufacturing and the next generation of economic growth. Electricity demand is surging for the first time in decades, and utilities are scrambling to keep up. But in the rush to modernize the grid, policymakers risk overlooking a simple question: Are we building new vulnerabilities into the very systems America will depend on most? 

Between the two of us, we have spent decades working on these issues from different perspectives. One of us served in Congress and helped build many of the cybersecurity institutions the federal government now relies on to protect critical infrastructure. The other spent years leading one of America’s largest energy companies and helping coordinate the electric sector’s response to cyber and physical threats. 

Over that time, we have seen the same mistake play out again and again: America focuses on cost and convenience until dependence on foreign technology suddenly becomes a national security problem. By then, fixing it is far more difficult than preventing it in the first place. After years of allowing the purchase of cheap Chinese telecommunications devices, for example, the Federal Communications Commission estimated it would cost more than $5 billion to remove and replace them with more secure equipment.

The electric grid is becoming more digital than most Americans realize. The systems being installed now do more than move electricity; they communicate with one another, process data and help utilities manage power across entire regions in real time. Those features make the grid more efficient, but they also create new points of vulnerability. 

Many Americans also may not know that Chinese government-linked hackers are already burrowing inside U.S. critical infrastructure, including power systems. U.S. officials have warned that these intrusions appear designed to give Beijing options to disrupt vital services during a future crisis. That should raise an obvious question: If China is already trying to gain access to critical systems from the outside, why would we make it easier by relying on Chinese-linked technology in the systems that help control the grid from the inside? 

Of course, that doesn’t mean every Chinese-made component poses the same risk. A screw, a solar panel and a battery cell — components that simply perform a particular function — present a very different challenge from the software and control systems that determine how electricity is generated, stored and routed.

That distinction should guide U.S. policy. Too often, Washington treats every component as if it poses the same threat. The greatest scrutiny should fall on the software, firmware and control systems that act as the grid’s command center. These technologies can communicate remotely, receive updates and influence how electricity flows across large parts of the country. They present a fundamentally different risk from commodity hardware. 

The U.S. cannot replace every foreign-made control system overnight. But failing to prioritize carries risks of its own. As a recent paper from two leading cyber research institutions explains, we can and must assess technologies based on their systemic importance, impact and level of digital sophistication, domestically sourcing the most important while relying on cheap global supplies for the least consequential.

America has already spent years trying to unwind vulnerabilities in industries such as semiconductors and telecommunications after strategic concerns took a back seat to short-term economic considerations. We should not make the same mistake with the systems that will power the next generation of American innovation. 

Neither government nor industry can solve this challenge alone. Most of America’s critical infrastructure is owned by the private sector, yet companies cannot be expected to independently determine which technologies pose the greatest national security concerns. Washington should provide clearer guidance about which systems deserve the closest scrutiny and share threat information more aggressively with the companies responsible for deploying them. Industry, in turn, should use that data to make smarter sourcing and procurement decisions before vulnerable technologies become deeply embedded across the grid. 

The decisions being made today will shape our nation’s energy security for decades. If we focus on the technologies that matter most, we can modernize the grid while reducing risk. If we fail to do so, we may discover too late that we built avoidable vulnerabilities into one of the country’s most important systems. 

Most Americans rarely think about the electric grid, because from an outside viewpoint, it just works. Our leaders should do everything possible to keep it that way. 

Jim Langevin is a former U.S. representative from Rhode Island. Tom Fanning is the former chairman and CEO of Southern Company. Both serve on the advisory board of the Foundation for Defense of Democracies’ Center on Cyber and Technology Innovation.

data.title: Watch live: Trump pick to lead CFPB faces Senate confirmation hearing
data.description:
Brian Johnson, President Trump’s pick for director of the Consumer Finance Protection Bureau (CFPB) is testifying Thursday morning before the Senate Banking Committee.

Johnson served in various roles at the agency during Trump’s first administration.

Office of Management and Budget Director Russell Vought has been serving as the acting CFPB head. Earlier this month, he was lambasted by Democrats over his tenure at the consumer watchdog agency, where he has moved quickly to halt many of its functions and shut down its headquarters.

The CFPB, which was created in the wake of the 2007-08 financial crisis, has long been a target for conservatives, who argue it is a prime example of government overreach.

The hearing, which includes a slew of other nominations, is scheduled to begin at 10 a.m. EDT.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: Trump says nuclear deal depends on Saudis normalizing Israel relations
data.description:
President Trump on Thursday said the nuclear agreement between the U.S. and Saudi Arabia is contingent on the Saudis normalizing relations with Israel through the Abraham Accords.

“The Civil Nuclear Deal (There will be no enrichment of material!) being made between the United States Department of Energy and Saudi Arabia, which pertains only to non-military use such as the ones that Iran and UAE (and others) already have, will be approved, but is totally subject to Saudi Arabia joining the very respected and successful Abraham Accords,” Trump wrote on Truth Social.

“The United States is not opposed to Civil (Non-Enriched) Nuclear Facilities,” he added.

Energy Department Secretary Chris Wright and his Saudi counterpart Prince Abdulaziz bin Salman signed the agreement Wednesday on civilian nuclear cooperation, along with a bilateral safeguards agreement. It would be a decades-long deal that would involve U.S. companies and billions of dollars.

Democratic lawmakers and nonproliferation experts have already expressed concern about the deal and what safeguards it contains, along with what this deal could mean for the region amid the Iran war.

The deal and its details have not been released. It will be sent to Congress for review.

Trump has long sought to add countries to his Abraham Accords, which were signed during his first term.

Saudi Arabia for its part has said that normalizing relations with Israel would first need clear progress towards a Palestinian state, something that Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu has dismissed.

The Israeli prime minister’s office said Thursday, “The joint American and Israeli military action against the genocidal regime in Teheran and Israel’s crushing of Iran’s terror axis have created the possibility to expand the circle of peace.”

“As President Trump has said, Saudi Arabia’s joining of the Abraham Accords would be an historic leap forward for peace in the Middle East,” the statement said. “Peace through Strength.”

Several countries have already signed the Abraham Accords, including the United Arab Emirates, Bahrain, Sudan and Morocco.

data.title: Homan on how ICE has improved since January
data.description:
White House border czar Tom Homan on Wednesday discussed how Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) has changed since January, when two U.S. citizens were killed in Minneapolis by federal immigration authorities.

“You know, I was honest with the American people, you know, in the last few months that the ICE arrest numbers are down because the department was shut down, right?” Homan said on NewsNation’s “Katie Pavlich Tonight.”

“The Democrats shut the department down mainly because of, you know, what occurred in Minneapolis. I was a part of the negotiation team on the hill. And everything they brought up — they wanted changes before they opened the government — was a reflection on Minnesota,” he added.

Earlier this year, the Trump administration faced backlash for its immigration operations in Minnesota, where Alex Pretti and Renee Good were killed by federal immigration authorities. Pretti and Good’s deaths inflamed already intense anger toward the administration over its immigration agenda.

In May, a record-long Department of Homeland Security (DHS) shutdown came to an end after months of negotiations between congressional Democrats and Republicans.

Democrats had opposed funding for ICE and Border Patrol, both under DHS, amid the Trump administration’s crackdown on immigration and following the deaths of Pretti and Good. In the agreement to end the shutdown, the two agencies did not receive additional funding.

In June, President Trump signed into law a congressional budget package to fund ICE and Border Control for the remainder of his term. The reconciliation funding legislation handed the agencies almost $70 million, which was split between ICE, Border Control and DHS’s discretionary spending budget.

“But, as soon as we got the reconciliation done, we got the funding, numbers are up. I mean, in June, we had the highest numbers of arrests in the history of the agency, of ICE. And July’s tracking to even beat that. So, we’re hitting on all cylinders now. We’re deportation-maxing. Since we’re fully funded, we just got to keep going,” Homan said Wednesday.

However, intense scrutiny toward ICE has returned in recent weeks, with two fatal shootings by the agency in Maine and Texas.

data.title: The Americas' common agenda has a clear voice: Marco Rubio
data.description:
It is no accident that Secretary of State Marco Rubio was among the first to embrace a hemispheric variation on the most famous slogan in American politics: “Make the Americas Great Again.”

What reads at first as wordplay in fact carries considerable weight, because it names something that has rarely been harder to deny. The agendas of the U.S. and much of Latin America are advancing, increasingly and perhaps inevitably, in the same direction, closing gaps that have often widened in the past. 

But this kind of alignment, however, does not happen on its own. It requires an articulator: someone with the political skill to translate shared interests into working policy. It would be simplistic to credit any single author for the alignment now taking shape across the hemisphere. But it is, in my view, unmistakable that Rubio has been central to it. 

In recent history, hardly anyone else at the highest administration level has a better knowledge and understanding of the Western Hemisphere. The son of Cuban immigrants and the first Latino to lead the State Department, he has given the Western Hemisphere the place in American strategic thinking that durable partnership requires: not an afterthought, not a problem file, but a political vision where the United States stands to benefit as much as its continental allies.  

That strategic realignment, led by Rubio, is visible in three developments that deserve close attention.

The first is the administration’s greater use of political ambassadors and, by extension, of embassies as full-fledged instruments of economic diplomacy. Where economic statecraft was once quarantined in specialized bureaus, it now runs through the missions’ chiefs themselves. By the secretary’s own account, American ambassadors’ engagements today concern very specific economic matters, from critical minerals to strategic infrastructure. 

Latin America is living proof of this new model. Missions across the region are fully staffed, and a notable share are led by political ambassadors with a direct line to the White House: Bernie Navarro in Peru, Kevin Marino Cabrera in Panamá and Peter Lamelas in Argentina, among others.

Taken together, they represent an investment that speaks volumes about where the hemisphere now sits in American strategic thinking. Cooperation grounded not in assistance but in the alignment of interests; a distinction the administration has come to grasp, and one Secretary Rubio has proven an effective messenger for.

The second is the substance of the common agenda itself. As the U.S. recalibrates its strategy toward the region, the real work of diplomacy lies in identifying the areas where U.S. and Latin American interests converge and translating those shared interests into practical cooperation — particularly through the implementation of the 2025 National Security Strategy.

In combating transnational crime, countries across the hemisphere increasingly view the disruption of illicit economies and transnational criminal organizations not as a rhetorical objective but as a shared operational priority.

Beyond security cooperation, there is also a common interest in preserving strategic balance in the region. This reflects the administration’s broader objective of shaping common priorities and strengthening hemispheric cooperation to advance regional security and stability, while also reinforcing energy security and resilient supply chains for critical U.S. industries, including defense.

Finally, underpinning all of this is the political momentum that has made such convergence possible. The administration has made no secret of its preference for the bilateral over the multilateral. Its interests are not up for negotiation, so it seeks out, directly, the countries that already share them.

In this sense, Latin America increasingly qualifies, shifting rightward with recent election victories by Keiko Fujimori in Peru, Jose Antonio Kast in Chile and Abelardo de la Espriella in Colombia. An electoral map guarantees nothing, but political affinity lowers friction and removes roadblocks. 

It is this convergence of factors that has enabled the Western Hemisphere. The U.S. will reclaim its position as the principal ally for Latin American governments, who now, more than ever, are willing to share the burden of regional security and stability.

But every repositioning of this magnitude needs someone to connect its pieces and, perhaps most importantly, to lead its purpose — and that role has fallen to Rubio, who has proven equal to it.   

Alfredo Ferrero is Peru’s ambassador to the United States.

data.title: Rubio: Trump military policy for Iran is 'head for an eye'
data.description:
Secretary of State Marco Rubio warned early Thursday that President Trump is prepared to direct an outsized military response to Iranian aggression as the two sides continue to exchange fire nearly five months into the conflict.

Rubio responded to Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi’s statement that Tehran’s policy is an “eye for an eye” in comments to reporters at The Association of Southeast Asian Countries’s 2026 summit in Manila, Philippines. 

“The president’s policy is a head for an eye,” he said. “I mean honestly, that’s what it’s going to be. I mean, they will pay a very heavy price for the things they’re doing.”

Araghchi issued the warning Wednesday in a post on social platform X.

“Our defense doctrine is clear: eye for an eye,” the foreign minister wrote. “Any aggression against Iran, including our infrastructure, will compel a powerful and decisive response.”

These threats follow a brief period of optimism last month that the war could soon reach a resolution after the U.S. and Iran signed a memorandum of understanding that initiated a halt in fighting and renewed peace deal talks. 

However, both Tehran and Washington have alleged that the other side violated the terms of this ceasefire deal and launched attacks in response. 

A rising death toll among U.S. troops and Iranian citizens has increased pressure on the Trump administration to wind down these military operations, with several Republican lawmakers expressing dissatisfaction with the way the president has handled this conflict. 

“There’s got to be an off-ramp, that’s all there is to it,” Sen. Jim Justice (R-W.Va.) told reporters on Wednesday. 

On Wednesday, Trump attended a dignified transfer of four American service members who were recently killed in Jordan and Iraq amid the war.

Rubio told reporters on Wednesday that Tehran is “probably not ready” to make a deal but that its leaders “will be soon.”

“Because the price they’re paying is very high … Despite all that bravado, they are suffering tremendously and they’re going to suffer more until they come to their senses,” he said. “They’re not there yet, unfortunately.”

Alexander Bolton contributed reporting.
